title: Fitting the STAGE Model
text: demonstrate workflow, simulate simple dataset lengths [L,U][L, U] smooth transition around true m50m_{50}.
code: N <- 200 L <- 1000 U <- 1500 true_m50 <- 1250 true_d   <- 100  # Simulate covariate x <- runif(N, L, U)  # Smooth transition in P(mature) p <- plogis((x - true_m50) / (true_d / 4))  # Binary maturity indicator y <- rbinom(N, 1, p)  head(tibble(x = x, y = y)) #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>       x     y #>   <dbl> <int> #> 1 1133.     0 #> 2 1186.     0 #> 3 1286.     1 #> 4 1454.     1 #> 5 1101.     0 #> 6 1449.     1

title: Fitting the STAGE Model
text: now fit STAGE model using fit_stage(). STAGE Bayesian model fit via Stan, keep chains iter small vignette keep runtime acceptable; real applications increase . can inspect posterior standard tools summary(fit$fit), bayesplot, posterior utilities want diagnostics.
code: fit <- fit_stage(x, y, L = L, U = U, chains = 2, iter = 1000) fit

title: Fitting the STAGE Model
text: helper transition_point() returns posterior summaries global transition point m50m_{50}. single-population fit, returns list single element global: named numeric vector posterior mean, median, q2.5, q97.5.
code: transition_point(fit)

title: Fitting the STAGE Model
text: can also predict Pr(mature∣x)\Pr(\text{mature} \mid x) grid lengths, plot posterior mean transition curve.
code: x_grid <- seq(L, U, length.out = 200)  p_hat <- predict(fit, x_grid, type = "prob")  plot(   x_grid, p_hat, type = "l",   xlab = "Length (x)",   ylab = "P(mature)",   las = 1 )

title: Fitting the STAGE Model
text: many applications multiple populations (e.g. regions, stocks, sexes) expect: shared global transition point m50m_{50}, population-specific deviations. STAGE implements using hierarchical structure 𝚖𝟻𝟶_𝚙𝚘𝚙[j]=m50+zjσα, \texttt{m50\_pop}[j] = m_{50} + z_j \,\sigma_\alpha,  non-centred parameterisation efficient sampling. simple two-group example: transition_point(fit2) returns list : global: posterior summary global m50m_{50} (mean, median, q2.5, q97.5), pop1, pop2, …: population-specific entries summaries m50_pop[j].
code: group <- rep(c("A", "B"), each = N/2)  fit2 <- fit_stage(   x, y,   group = group,   L = L, U = U,   chains = 2, iter = 1000 )  transition_point(fit2)

title: Fitting the STAGE Model
text: vignette : introduced plateau–Gaussian likelihood underlying STAGE, visualised class densities resulting transition probabilities, simulated simple dataset, fit STAGE model estimate transition point, extended model hierarchical multi-population setting. practice, want : choose [L,U][L, U] capture transition zone plus plateau side, check posterior diagnostics (divergences, R-hat, effective sample sizes), interpret m50m_{50} uncertainty biological context. Future vignettes expand : comparing STAGE logistic regression Gaussian LDA, sensitivity choice [L,U][L, U] prior settings.

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: vignette describes likelihood parameterisation used STAGE model. STAGE hierarchical Bayesian generative model designed estimate transition point m50m_{50} variable xx two classes—example, length individuals transition immature mature. model produces posterior distribution m50m_{50}, representing value xx probabilities Pr(y=0)\Pr(y=0) Pr(y=1)\Pr(y=1) equal. model can also used derive useful quantities (class probabilities Pr(y=1∣x)\Pr(y = 1 \mid x)). Conceptually, STAGE Bayesian generative classifier estimating transition point (e.g. length maturity). class fit asymmetric plateau–Gaussian density: lower class uniform plateau followed Gaussian tail transition, higher class Gaussian tail transition followed uniform plateau.  construction focuses inference overlap region prevents distant observations (e.g. small immatures, large matures) unduly influencing transition point. central idea model class piecewise density combines uniform plateau regions far transition, Gaussian tail transition region. left group (y=0y = 0) modelled uniform left begins lower truncation value LL. distribution remains flat approaches transition range, point distribution declines according Gaussian. right group (y=1y = 1) mirror image left—increases according Gaussian distribution levels uniform, terminating upper truncation value UU. model focuses inference transition zone, values xx two classes overlap, -weights observations biologically uninformative far away transition point. uniform–Gaussian pdf used adapted plateau proposal distribution combining uniform two Gaussians described Lau & Krumscheid (2022).

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: observe pairs (xi,yi)(x_i, y_i) =1,…,Ni = 1, \dots, N, xix_i continuous covariate (e.g., length), truncated interval [L,U][L, U], yi∈{0,1}y_i \\{0, 1\} binary indicator state (e.g., immature / mature). model assumes distribution xx conditional state piecewise: y=0y = 0 (immature individuals), distribution xx approximately uniform point μ0\mu_0, follows Gaussian decay. y=1y = 1 (mature individuals), distribution Gaussian point μ1\mu_1, uniform μ1\mu_1 upper truncation UU. transition point m50m_{50} value xx probability observing either class equal: Pr(y=0∣x=m50)=Pr(y=1∣x=m50). \Pr(y = 0 \mid x = m_{50}) = \Pr(y = 1 \mid x = m_{50}). Additional parameters govern width sharpness transition: dd controls distance μ0\mu_0 μ1\mu_1 (width transition region), σ\sigma (denoted sigma_x Stan code) controls spread (standard deviation) Gaussian tails. allowing different behaviours either side transition, model can capture asymmetric transition patterns: immature class can decline towards transition different rate mature class rises . particularly useful settings : boundary classes sharp, data exhibit gradual overlap two classes.

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: likelihood built two unnormalised log-density functions two classes. work log scale numerical stability subtract log-normalising constants obtain valid densities. Let ℓ0(x)\ell_0(x) ℓ1(x)\ell_1(x) denote unnormalised log-densities y=0y = 0 y=1y = 1, respectively. corresponding (unnormalised) densities f̃k(x)=exp{ℓk(x)}\tilde f_k(x) = \exp\{\ell_k(x)\}. restrict attention truncated interval [L,U][L, U]; densities zero outside range.

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: yi=0y_i = 0, unnormalised piecewise log-density ℓ0(xi∣μ0,σ,L)={0if L≤xi≤μ0,−(xi−μ0)22σ2if xi>μ0. \ell_0(x_i \mid \mu_0, \sigma, L) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{} L \leq x_i \leq \mu_0, \\ -\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma^2} & \text{} x_i > \mu_0 . \end{cases} : plateau region [L,μ0][L, \mu_0] constant log-density 0 (density 1), tail region xi>μ0x_i > \mu_0 decays according Gaussian kernel. constant added ℓ0(⋅)\ell_0(\cdot) without changing posterior; fix plateau 0 plateau density 1 exponentiation.

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: yi=1y_i = 1, unnormalised log-density ℓ1(xi∣μ1,σ,U)={−(xi−μ1)22σ2if xi<μ1,0if μ1≤xi≤U. \ell_1(x_i \mid \mu_1, \sigma, U) = \begin{cases} -\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma^2} & \text{} x_i < \mu_1, \\ 0 & \text{} \mu_1 \leq x_i \leq U . \end{cases} Now: Gaussian tail μ1\mu_1, plateau covers upper region [μ1,U][\mu_1, U]. cases plateau–Gaussian shape: flat density class “dominates” Gaussian tail transition region class present.

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: convert f̃k(x)=exp{ℓk(x)}\tilde f_k(x) = \exp\{\ell_k(x)\} proper probability density functions [L,U][L, U], divide constants C0C_0 C1C_1 chosen densities integrate 1. immature class (y=0y = 0): C0=∫Lμ01dx+∫μ0∞exp(−(x−μ0)22σ2)dx=(μ0−L)+2πσ2. C_0 = \int_L^{\mu_0} 1 \, dx   + \int_{\mu_0}^{\infty}     \exp\!\left(       -\frac{(x - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma^2}     \right) dx = (\mu_0 - L) + \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}{2}. mature class (y=1y = 1): C1=∫−∞μ1exp(−(x−μ1)22σ2)dx+∫μ1U1dx=2πσ2+(U−μ1). C_1 = \int_{-\infty}^{\mu_1}     \exp\!\left(       -\frac{(x - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma^2}     \right) dx   + \int_{\mu_1}^{U} 1 \, dx = \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}{2} + (U - \mu_1). Intuitively: (μ0−L)(\mu_0 - L) (U−μ1)(U - \mu_1) widths uniform plateaus, 2πσ/2\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma / 2 contribution half-Gaussian tail. corresponding log-likelihood contribution observation ii logℒi={ℓ0(xi)−logC0if yi=0,ℓ1(xi)−logC1if yi=1. \log \mathcal{L}_i = \begin{cases} \ell_0(x_i) - \log C_0 & \text{} y_i = 0, \\ \ell_1(x_i) - \log C_1 & \text{} y_i = 1. \end{cases} Summing observations, logp(x,y∣m50,d,σ)=∑=1N[ℓyi(xi)−logCyi], \log p(x, y \mid m_{50}, d, \sigma) = \sum_{=1}^{N} \big[   \ell_{y_i}(x_i) - \log C_{y_i} \big], exactly likelihood implemented Stan code used fit_stage().

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: priors reflect prior beliefs plausible transition points transition widths typical length--maturity problems. practice, defaults adapted scale data prior biological knowledge. default, STAGE uses weakly informative normal priors: Transition point m50m_{50} (Stan parameter m50): m50∼𝒩(1300,50), m_{50} \sim \mathcal{N}(1300, 50), centres transition point around 1300 (mm) moderate uncertainty. Transition width dd: d∼𝒩(100,100), d \sim \mathcal{N}(100, 100), allows width overlap region vary around 100 units wide spread. Note dd distance class-specific Gaussian centres. Gaussian spread σ\sigma (Stan parameter sigma_x): σ∼𝒩(100,50), \sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(100, 50), representing prior beliefs scale variability transition tails. dd sigma_x constrained positive Stan model, Normal priors effectively half-Normal distributions (prior mass zero folded onto positive half). practice, adapt priors scale (e.g., lengths, ages) prior biological knowledge. STAGE implementation allows pass hyperparameters via helper functions stage_priors().

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: Gaussian “centres” two classes defined terms transition point m50m_{50} width parameter dd: μ0=m50−d2,μ1=m50+d2. \mu_0 = m_{50} - \frac{d}{2}, \qquad \mu_1 = m_{50} + \frac{d}{2}. Thus: dd controls distance immature mature Gaussian means, m50m_{50} midpoint .

what: From class densities to class probabilities (Bayes rule)
title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: likelihood defines class-conditional densities f0(x)=p(x∣y=0)f_0(x) = p(x \mid y = 0) f1(x)=p(x∣y=1)f_1(x) = p(x \mid y = 1) [L,U][L, U], obtained normalising unnormalised densities f̃k(x)=exp{ℓk(x)}\tilde f_k(x) = \exp\{\ell_k(x)\} constants CkC_k. obtain probability individual particular xx value higher class (y=1y = 1), use Bayes rule: Pr(y=1∣x)=π1f1(x)π0f0(x)+π1f1(x), \Pr(y = 1 \mid x) = \frac{\pi_1 f_1(x)}{\pi_0 f_0(x) + \pi_1 f_1(x)}, similarly Pr(y=0∣x)=π0f0(x)π0f0(x)+π1f1(x), \Pr(y = 0 \mid x) = \frac{\pi_0 f_0(x)}{\pi_0 f_0(x) + \pi_1 f_1(x)}, π0\pi_0 π1\pi_1 prior class probabilities (e.g., prior probabilities immature mature seeing xx). many applications, estimating length--maturity, interested transition point driven sample sizes prevalence, natural use equal class priors, π0=π1=1/2\pi_0 = \pi_1 = 1/2. case, Bayes rule simplifies Pr(y=1∣x)=f1(x)f0(x)+f1(x),Pr(y=0∣x)=f0(x)f0(x)+f1(x). \Pr(y = 1 \mid x) = \frac{f_1(x)}{f_0(x) + f_1(x)}, \qquad \Pr(y = 0 \mid x) = \frac{f_0(x)}{f_0(x) + f_1(x)}. class probabilities STAGE model uses prediction, see plot Pr(y=1∣x)\Pr(y = 1 \mid x) function xx.

previous_headings: From class densities to class probabilities (Bayes rule)
title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: definition, transition point m50m_{50} value xx two classes equally probable: Pr(y=0∣x=m50)=Pr(y=1∣x=m50)=12. \Pr(y = 0 \mid x = m_{50}) = \Pr(y = 1 \mid x = m_{50}) = \frac{1}{2}. Using Bayes rule equal class priors, equality equivalent f0(m50)=f1(m50), f_0(m_{50}) = f_1(m_{50}), .e. two normalised class densities equal m50m_{50}. m50m_{50} midpoint μ0\mu_0 μ1\mu_1. transition region (μ0<x<μ1\mu_0 < x < \mu_1), unnormalised densities take Gaussian form: f̃0(x)=exp(−(x−μ0)22σ2),f̃1(x)=exp(−(x−μ1)22σ2). \tilde f_0(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \qquad \tilde f_1(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right). Since Gaussians share σ\sigma, unnormalised densities equal arithmetic midpoint x*=(μ0+μ1)/2=m50x^* = (\mu_0 + \mu_1)/2 = m_{50}. equal class priors, normalising constants C0C_0 C1C_1 cancel likelihood ratio, 50% crossing point exactly m50m_{50} regardless survey bounds LL UU. key invariance property: changing sampling domain shift estimated transition point.

title: The STAGE Likelihood
text: stage package: single-population model directly samples m50 (m50m_{50}), d, sigma_x (σ\sigma). transition point primary sampling parameter, derived quantity. multi-population (hierarchical) model introduces population-specific transition points via random-effects structure: 𝚖𝟻𝟶_𝚙𝚘𝚙[j]=m50+zjσα, \texttt{m50\_pop}[j] = m_{50} + z_j \, \sigma_\alpha,  m50m_{50} global mean transition point, zjz_j standard-normal effects, σα\sigma_\alpha population-level standard deviation. Stan code used fit_stage() direct implementation likelihood parameterisation described . Future vignettes provide detailed walk-Stan code extensions alternative priors model comparison.

title: Motivation

Given data , can easily identify broad “transition zone”, transition class 0 class 1 takes place. However, specific goal estimate “transition point”, m50m_{50}—value xx probability class 1 reaches 50%. m̂50={x:Pr̂(y=1∣x)=0.5}\hat{m}_{50} = \{x : \widehat{\Pr}(y = 1 \mid x) = 0.5\} maturity studies, point might called “length 50% maturity”. left m50m_{50}, class 0 likely; right m50m_{50}, class 1 likely. critical point m50m_{50} represents: biological property individual, property sampled population. question asked : given individual length xx, length evidence length alone make maturity probable immaturity? answer depends length-conditional distributions mature immature individuals—nothing many sampled. practice, sampling almost always biased respect class: gear selectivity, habitat preferences, variation sampling effort routinely produce samples different proportions mature immature individuals, none reflects underlying biology. valid estimator m50m_{50} must independent sampling vagaries.

Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) widely used generative classifier fits Gaussian (Normal) density class. approach less sensitive class imbalance, can suffer another important limitation: estimates transition points LDA (generative models) potentially influenced values xx, just near transition zone. Observations far transition zone greatly affect fitted density class, therefore undue influence estimate transition point. example, true transition around 1200 mm, individuals 200–400 mm influence estimate maturity transition. LDA particular, symmetrical Gaussian distributions fit class, behaviour tails near transition determined much lowest highest values xx near transition. context length--maturity, means small immature individuals (e.g., neonates) large mature individuals much influence transition point individuals whose lengths fall near maturity boundary. unappealing, distant observations contain almost information location transition, yet shape Gaussian fits can shift inferred transition point substantially. Instead, tail constrained mirror image opposite tail class (global, class-wise, Gaussian assumption).

title: Motivation
text: STAGE model Bayesian generative classifier class fit asymmetric Uniform–Gaussian mixture. Immature (y = 0): uniform plateau → Gaussian decay approaching transition. Mature (y = 1): Gaussian rise transition → uniform plateau.  STAGE model following key properties: Asks right biological question STAGE estimates m50m_{50} point two class-conditional densities equal, using equal class priors. directly answers question interest: length individual unknown maturity status equally likely mature immature, based length alone? class priors fixed π0=π1=12\pi_0 = \pi_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} depend sample composition, estimate m50m_{50} construction independent class imbalance data. Gear selectivity, habitat-driven sampling bias, variation field effort distort estimate, prevalence class sample plays role likelihood. merely robustness imbalance—immunity, follows directly asking right question. Focus data transition zone Observations far transition zone enter via uniform component, little influence estimate transition point. Individuals provide information boundary contribute constant term likelihood. Decline one class matched increase many transition problems (length maturity), density lower class must decline exactly region higher-class density increases. STAGE encodes fitting shared standard deviation upper tail lower class lower tail higher class. jointly modelling tails, change one class’s decline implies matching change ’s rise. ensures estimated transition point driven relative behaviour two densities, marginal behaviour either class . Bayesian inference STAGE presented Bayesian paradigm, providing flexibility incorporate prior information quantify uncertainty derived parameter quantity. Multi-population structure handled naturally hierarchical model: uncertainty densities propagates directly uncertainty m50m_{50}, population-level patterns can estimated efficiently. STAGE likelihood structured mixture inspired plateau–Gaussian formulation Lau & Krumscheid (2022), adapted specifically transition-point estimation.

title: Simulation Study Design
text: goal simulation study evaluate performance STAGE model estimating biological transition points (e.g. length 50% maturity, m50m_{50}) competing methods, range conditions reflecting real-world data collection challenges. study demonstrate STAGE performs well, degrades gracefully, assumptions violated.

title: Simulation Study Design
text: methods described implemented stage package, providing consistent interface comparable Bayesian inference across methods. m50m_{50} extracted methods 50% crossing point equal class priors (π0=π1=0.5\pi_0 = \pi_1 = 0.5). Frequentist logistic regression (MLE) also included baseline, fitted via glm().
code: library(stage)  # STAGE — plateau-Gaussian generative classifier (this package) fit_stage(x, y, L = L, U = U)  # Bayesian LDA — equal-sigma Gaussian class-conditionals, pi fixed at 0.5 fit_lda(x, y)  # Bayesian logistic regression — standard discriminative model fit_logistic(x, y)  # Bayesian logistic regression with full inverse-probability weights fit_logistic(x, y, weights = "full")  # Bayesian logistic regression with square-root inverse-probability weights fit_logistic(x, y, weights = "sqrt")

title: Simulation Study Design
text: Maturity status assigned probabilistically via logistic transition function length xx: Pr(y=1∣x)=logistic(slope×(x−m50))\Pr(y = 1 \mid x) = \text{logistic}(\text{slope} \times (x - m_{50})) discriminative truth — Pr(y=1∣x)\Pr(y = 1 \mid x) defined directly rather via generative model. STAGE therefore tested mild model misspecification, relevant real-world condition. secondary set scenarios simulate STAGE generative model directly (drawing xx plateau-Gaussian class-conditionals) test performance correctly specified model. scenarios clearly labelled.

title: Simulation Study Design
code: m50_true   <- 1250   # mm — true transition point slope_true <- 0.08   # logistic slope; roughly equivalent to d=150, sigma=50 L_pop      <- 200    # true lower bound of population length distribution U_pop      <- 2000   # true upper bound

title: Simulation Study Design
text: Real samples iid draws population length distribution. Sampling patchy: fish taken discrete events (hauls, trawl sets, survey stations) locations may favour particular size class; gear selectivity imposes length-dependent catchability; cohort structure means fish single event may highly similar length. two-level sampling model captures non-independence: degree clustering controlled event_mu_sd relative length_sd. event_mu_sd small, sample approximately iid. simpler iid baseline also used:
code: simulate_clustered <- function(     n_events,       # number of fishing events     n_per_event,    # fish per event (scalar or vector)     m50, slope,     # true biological parameters     event_mu_mean,  # mean of event-level length modes     event_mu_sd,    # SD across events (spatial/cohort variation)     length_sd,      # within-event SD (individual variation)     L, U            # analyst-specified truncation bounds ) {   event_means <- rnorm(n_events, mean = event_mu_mean, sd = event_mu_sd)   x <- unlist(mapply(     function(mu, n) rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = length_sd),     mu = event_means,     n  = rep(n_per_event, length.out = n_events)   ))   p <- plogis(slope * (x - m50))   y <- rbinom(length(x), size = 1, prob = p)   keep <- x >= L & x <= U   tibble::tibble(x = x[keep], y = y[keep]) } simulate_simple <- function(n0, n1, m50, slope, L, U) {   x <- c(runif(n0, L, m50), runif(n1, m50, U))   p <- plogis(slope * (x - m50))   y <- rbinom(n0 + n1, size = 1, prob = p)   tibble::tibble(x = x, y = y) }

title: Simulation Study Design

code: truncation_scenarios <- tibble::tribble(   ~label,        ~L,    ~U,    ~notes,   "Full range",  200,   2000,  "Baseline; wide plateaus",   "Moderate",    500,   1700,  "Well outside transition zone",   "Tight",       900,   1600,  "Narrower plateaus; still outside",   "Marginal",    1100,  1400,  "Very narrow plateaus; near mu0/mu1",   "Stress test", 1150,  1350,  "Inside transition zone; expected failure",   # Asymmetric — tests L/U invariance claim   "Tight lower", 1100,  2000,  "L near mu0, U wide",   "Tight upper", 200,   1400,  "L wide, U near mu1" )

title: Simulation Study Design
text: JJ populations varying m50m_{50}, using fit_stage(..., group = ...): Compare hierarchical STAGE fitting STAGE independently population. hierarchical model demonstrate partial pooling: small populations borrow strength larger ones.
code: hierarchical_scenarios <- tibble::tribble(   ~label,             ~J,  ~sigma_alpha, ~n_per_pop,   "Few, similar",     5,   20,           100,   "Few, divergent",   5,   100,          100,   "Many, similar",    20,  20,           100,   "Many, unbalanced", 20,  50,           "Poisson(50)" )

title: Simulation Study Design
text: Across R=500R = 500 simulated datasets per scenario: Bias variance reported separately, just RMSE — method can good RMSE trading bias variance, different kinds failure. Bayesian methods, m50_hat posterior median. Coverage nominal 95% level key diagnostic uncertainty quantification. R=500R = 500 replicates, SE coverage estimate 0.95 approximately 0.010, giving reasonable precision.
code: compute_metrics <- function(m50_hat, m50_true, lower, upper) {   tibble::tibble(     bias     = mean(m50_hat) - m50_true,     variance = var(m50_hat),     rmse     = sqrt(mean((m50_hat - m50_true)^2)),     coverage = mean(lower <= m50_true & m50_true <= upper),     width    = mean(upper - lower)   ) }

title: Simulation Study Design
text: methods paper, primary factors : Class imbalance (factor 2) — central claim, must thorough Sample size (factor 1) — necessary context Truncation (factor 3) — robustness claim honest characterisation boundary conditions Clustering (factor 4) — biological realism, novel test Factors 5–7 supplementary.

title: Simulation Study Design
text: Store thinned posterior draws, just summaries, allow retrospective analysis Prior choices fixed across scenarios documented explicitly — tune priors per scenario truncation scenarios, LL UU passed STAGE set truncation bounds used sampling, true population bounds Stress-test truncation scenarios clearly flagged boundary conditions
code: # Fix global seed; derive per-replicate seeds from it set.seed(42) sim_seeds <- sample.int(1e6, size = 500)  # Precompile Stan models before simulation loop # (compilation is slow; sampling is fast once compiled) model_stage    <- stage:::get_cmdstan_model("stage_Jpop.stan") model_lda      <- stage:::get_cmdstan_model("lda.stan") model_logistic <- stage:::get_cmdstan_model("logistic.stan")  # Parallelise across replicates future::plan(future::multisession) results <- furrr::future_map(sim_seeds, run_one_replicate, .options =   furrr::furrr_options(seed = TRUE))

title: Simulation Study Design

code: @Manual{,   title = {STAGE: State Transition Analysis via Generative Estimator},   author = {Adam N. H. Smith},   year = {2025},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/anhsmith/stage}, }

title: Models for Estimating Stage-Transition Points
text: stage package implements STAGE model (State Transition Analysis via Generative Estimator), Bayesian generative classifier estimating transition points (m50) two groups—value xx yy become probable. example length--maturity problems, yy binary maturity status, xx continuous measurement, length. Existing methods sorts applications important limitations (see ), STAGE model overcomes. Features STAGE model : class fit mixture distribution combines uniform plateau Gaussian tail Estimation transition point (m50) based relative densities (Bayes’ rule) classes given xx, discriminative regression shared transition region centred m50 width d. goal model transition-point estimation focuses inference data around transition point robust unbalanced sample sizes.  process testing STAGE model methods underway.

title: Models for Estimating Stage-Transition Points
text: fit_stage() — fit STAGE model (single multi-population) transition_point() — summarise posteriors m50 predict.stage_fit() — predict class probabilities labels stage_priors() — prior helper get_cmdstan_model() — internal CmdStan compiler helper Planned additions: Reparameterised logistic/HOF model Bayesian LDA Posterior predictive classification Diagnostic + visualisation tools Full vignette

title: Models for Estimating Stage-Transition Points
text: need cmdstanr + CmdStan installed: install GitHub:
code: install.packages("cmdstanr",   repos = c("https://mc-stan.org/r-packages/", getOption("repos")) ) cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()   # run once devtools::install_github("anhsmith/stage")

title: Models for Estimating Stage-Transition Points
text: Estimated transition point: Prediction:
code: library(stage)  set.seed(123)  N <- 200 L <- 1000 U <- 1500 true_m50 <- 1250 true_d   <- 100  # simulate data x <- runif(N, L, U) p <- plogis((x - true_m50) / (true_d / 4)) y <- rbinom(N, 1, p)  # fit STAGE model fit <- fit_stage(x, y, L = L, U = U, chains = 2, iter = 1000) fit transition_point(fit) x_grid <- seq(L, U, length.out = 100) p_hat <- predict(fit, x_grid, type = "prob")  plot(x_grid, p_hat, type = "l",      xlab = "x", ylab = "P(mature)", las = 1)

title: Models for Estimating Stage-Transition Points
text: Multiple populations: returns: global transition point m50 population-specific transition points m50_pop[j]
code: group <- rep(c("A","B"), each = N/2)  fit2 <- fit_stage(x, y, group = group, L = L, U = U)  transition_point(fit2)

title: Models for Estimating Stage-Transition Points
text: class uniform plateau Gaussian edge. Key parameters: m50 — transition point P(y=1|x) = 0.5 (direct sampling parameter) d — width transition region sigma_x — Gaussian spread Hierarchical structure: (non-centred parameterisation efficient sampling)
code: m50_pop[j] = m50 + z[j] * sigma_alpha

title: Fit the STAGE (uniform + Gaussian) generative model — fit_stage
text: x Numeric vector predictor values (e.g. length). y Binary vector: 0 = immature, 1 = mature. group Optional grouping variable (population). NULL, data treated one population (J = 1). L, U Truncation bounds x. NULL, use min(x) max(x). priors list prior hyperparameters created stage_priors(). chains, iter, ... Passed cmdstanr::sample().

title: Construct prior hyperparameters for the STAGE model — stage_priors
code: stage_priors(   x = NULL,   prior_m50_mu = if (!is.null(x)) stats::median(x) else 0,   prior_m50_tau = if (!is.null(x)) diff(range(x))/6 else 100,   prior_d_mu = if (!is.null(x)) diff(range(x))/4 else 0,   prior_d_tau = if (!is.null(x)) diff(range(x))/4 else 100,   prior_sigma_x_tau = if (!is.null(x)) stats::sd(x) else 100,   prior_sigma_alpha_tau = if (!is.null(x)) diff(range(x))/6 else 100 )

title: Construct prior hyperparameters for the STAGE model — stage_priors
text: x Optional data vector set sensible defaults. prior_m50_mu Mean prior global transition point m50. prior_m50_tau SD prior global transition point m50. prior_d_mu Mean prior d. prior_d_tau SD prior d. prior_sigma_x_tau SD scale sigma_x. prior_sigma_alpha_tau SD scale sigma_alpha (-pop).

title: Posterior summaries of STAGE transition points (m50) — transition_point.stage_fit
text: object stage_fit object returned fit_stage(). population Optional population index (integer) label (character). NULL, return global populations. ... Currently ignored. Included method compatibility.

title: Posterior summaries of STAGE transition points (m50) — transition_point.stage_fit
text: named list. Always contains global (posterior summary global m50). J > 1 models, also contains pop1, pop2, etc. population-specific transition point. summary named numeric vector mean, median, q2.5, q97.5.
